cells.text: To evaluate the treatment effect of PPS on knee pain in participants with knee OA pain.
• Change from baseline (CfB) at Day 112 in knee pain as assessed by the weekly average of daily pain (WADP) score on the numerical rating scale (NRS) 11-point (0–10) scale.
To evaluate the treatment effect of PPS on knee pain and function in participants with knee OA pain.

• Change from baseline (CfB) at Day 112 in function as assessed by the average functional subscale score of the WOMAC NRS 3.1 Index (for EMA/MHRA, function will be included as a co- primaryprimary endpoint but still tested in hierarchical order). • Change from baseline (CfB) at Day 112 in knee pain as assessed by the average pain subscale score of the WOMAC NRS 3.1 Index.
To evaluate the effect of PPS treatment on PGIC in participants with knee OA pain.
• Patient Global Impression of Change (PGIC) scores at Day 112.
To evaluate the efficacy of PPS treatment on knee pain, function, stiffness, and overall, in participants with knee OA pain.

Pain • Proportion of participants with ≥30% pain reduction as assessed by the average pain subscale score of the WOMAC NRS 3.1 Index at Day 112. Function • Proportion of participants with ≥30% improvement in function as assessed by the average functional subscale score of the WOMAC NRS 3.1 Index from baseline at Day 112.
See end of table notes also
Review OA history and previous OA medication/non-pharmacological treatment use
Confirm all medications and non-pharmacological treatment (e.g.,. vitamins, physiotherapy, TENS, etc) used previously for knee OA.
Collected at first Screening visit for evaluation of Inclusion Criteria 5 (Section 5.1). Diary will confirm eligibility to continue with screening activities.
Daily ADP NRS 0–10 pain score at least 14 days prior to randomisation

To be recorded between 6:00 pm00pm and 11:59 pm59pm daily, for at least the 14 days prior to randomisation. Site to check on Day 8 to ensure participant compliance and re-train participant as necessary.
Participant must remove their shoes before measurement of height and body weight.
Participant must rest supine for at least 5 minutes before vital signs (Section 8.2.2) and ECG (Section 8.2.3) measurements.

Fixed flexion PA (Section 8.6). If participant has two knees diagnosed with OA (Inclusion Criteria 12), and the pain evaluation on both knees at screening is +/-1 (ADP) then X- rayray both knees individually for K-L grade to determine index knee.
If participant is allergic to or cannot tolerate Gadolinium, please or site does not have access to perform a Gadolinium MRI, conduct MRI without Gadolinium.

For night shift workers, please contactcollect the medical monitor prior to collecting morning cortisol according to the workers corresponding “morning” hours.

US only: Only if morning cortisol is >3 µg/dL and <10 µg/dL. Participant must be sitting for collection of samples. For all participants if cortisol is <3 µg/dL (US) or <83 nmol/L (non-US)., ACTH stimulation following low cortisol result from Day 39 should be conducted within 7 days of receiving the cortisol result. If the test cannot be conducted within 7 days collect morning cortisol, ACTH, PRC/PRA, Aldosterone and DHEA-sS at the same visit. Participant must be sitting for collection of samples. Sample collection at time zero before stimulation and at 30-minute (+/- 2)-minute post stimulation, and 60- (+/-2)-minute post stimulation. US only: Collect also if cortisol is ≥3 µg/dL and <10 µg/dL. See Section 08.2.4.1.1.3.

be sent to a specialist for assessment Pigmentary Maculopathy and an FAF.

Ophthalmology Assessment (Comprehensive retinal exam, CFP, OCT and if available FAF)

Any participant that reports vision changes during the study should undergo an ophthalmology assessment. Assessment requirements to be discussed with Medical Monitor.All participants must have a full ophthalmologic review at Days 168 and 404 (or ET visit). Any participant who has symptoms suggestive of Pigmentary Maculophathy on available imaging and has not had an FAF should be sent to a specialist for assessment Pigmentary Maculopathy and an FAF.
Ophthalmology Assessment (Comprehensive retinal exam, CFP, OCT and if available FAF

All participants Any participant who has symptoms suggestive of Pigmentary Maculophathy on available imaging and has not had an FAF should be sent to a specialist for assessment Pigmentary Maculopathy and an FAF.
Minimum 20 patientsparticipants in each group, performed at selected sites.
If gadolinium was not used for screening MRI, do not use for follow-up MRI’s
PPS may cause pain, small amount of bleeding, bruising, swelling at injection site.

• Administration to be done by trained site staff. • Participant stopping rules for severe (Grade 3 or higher) injection site reactions (Section 7.1.1.2.2). • Participant monitoring for moderate (Grade 2 or less) injection site reactions (Section 8.3.7).
Increased liver enzyme concentrations, mild to moderate in PPS participants with return to baseline after PPS discontinuation.

• Regular monitoring and review of liver enzyme concentrations including ALT/AST, ALP, total serum bilirubin and INR. (Section 8.2.4). • Exclusion of participants with AST or ALT ≥2 ULN, total bilirubin ≥1.5 ULN and hepatitis B or C (Section 5.2). • Participant stopping rules (Section 7.1.1.2).
Thrombocytopenia, with or without symptoms of thrombosis, including HIT
Reported with PPS treatment of patients with OA (see IB).

• Exclusion of participants with history of increased bleeding, major bleeding episodes, and/or idiopathic or immune-mediated thrombocytopenia, previous heparin exposure within the last 100 days, history of thrombotic events, and taking anticoagulant or antiplatelet therapy (Section 5.2). • Frequent monitoring and review of platelets, haematology, and coagulation parameters (Section 1.3). • Management guidelines for the occurrence of thrombocytopenia (Section 7.1.1.2.1.1). • Participant stopping rules (Section 7.1.1.2).
PPS may cause an allergic reaction.

• Exclusion of participants with history of hypersensitivity to PPS, heparin or heparin-like drugs, or drugs of a similar chemical or pharmacological class (Section 5.2). • Monitoring of participants at site for 30 minutes after IP administration (Section 1.3).
Haemorrhage/ increased risk of bleeding may occur with increased aPTT/INR
PPS is a weak anticoagulant (see IB).

• Exclusion of participants taking anticoagulant or antiplatelet therapy, aPTT >36 s or >ULN (whichever is greater), or platelets <150,000 platelets/µL (Section 5.2). • Exclusion of bleeding disorders including haemophilia, cerebral bleeding and specific operations (Section 5.2). • Regular monitoring and review of haematology and coagulation parameters (Section 1.3). • Participant stopping rules (Section 7.1.1.2).

To date, PPS has not been associated with clinical signs or symptoms of primary adrenal insufficiency. Transient adrenal suppression may be seen with exogenous medications in the study population. The risk of adrenal effects is considered very low/theoretical; adrenal lesions seen in nonclinical rat studies were not associated with impaired adrenal function.

• Monitoring of adrenal hormone levels (Section 1.3). • Participant stopping rules (Section 7.1.1.2). • Monitoring for signs and symptoms of adrenal insufficiency (Section 8.2.4.1.1). • Assessment algorithms for abnormal adrenal results (Section 8.2.4.1.1.2).

Observed in some patients receiving chronic, oral PPS therapy (Pearce et al., 2018 [17]).Observed in some patients receiving chronic, oral PPS therapy (Pearce et al., 2018 [17]). Observed in a few (n=3) patients having received unregistered subcutaneous PPS at doses and lengths significantly higher and longer (>7 years) than this trial (Fung et al., 2025 [18]).

• Exclusion of participants if evidence of pigmentary maculopathy during Screening (Section 5.25.25.2). • Funduscopic retinal examinations if participants report vision changes during the study to identify any pigmentary changes of the retina from Screening (Section 1.3). • Funduscopic retinal examinations for all participants at Day 168 and Day 404 (Section 1.3). • Referral to an ophthalmologist for evaluation if clinically required (Section 8.2.4).8.2.6).
May cause pain, small amount of bleeding, bruising, swelling at venepuncture site.
• Trained site staff will collect blood samples and blood sampling is limited to those considered essential for participant safety and assessment of endpoints.
Potential allergic reaction to gadolinium. Precautions required if patient has certain implanted and/or internal metal objects (e.g.,. pacemakers).

• Applicable only to participants who have no contraindication to MRI or allergy or contraindication to gadolinium contrast agents. • Participants with an allergy or contraindication to gadolinium contrast agents will have the MRI without the administration of gadolinium for safety assessments. (Section 1.3). • Staff will ask participants about/check for implanted and/or internal metal objects.
Precautions required if patient has certain implanted
• Staff will ask participants about/check for implanted and/or internal metal objects.
• X-ray only for radiographic diagnosis and comparison of joint space width.
Use of ACTH stimulation test with tetracosactide

May cause pain, small amount of bleeding, bruising, swelling at venepuncture site. May cause an allergic reaction which may include redness or pain at the injection site, rash, itching, hives or flushing of the skin, dizziness, nausea (feeling sick) or vomiting, difficulty breathing, swelling of the face, lips, tongue, or other part of the body.

• Trained site staff will collect blood samples and blood sampling is limited to those considered essential for participant safety and assessment of endpoints. • Exclusion of participants with allergy or contraindication to tetracosactide (Section 5.2).

May cause gastrointestinal bleeding, kidney and liver damage (in combination with alcohol abuse). It is also associated with an increased risk of heart attack, stroke and high blood pressure (Roberts et al., 2016 [18]).May cause gastrointestinal bleeding, kidney and liver damage (in combination with alcohol abuse). It is also associated with an increased risk of heart attack, stroke and high BP (Roberts et al., 2016 [18]).
To evaluate the treatment effect of PPS on knee pain in participants with knee OA pain.
• Change from baseline (CfB) at Day 112 in knee pain as assessed by the weekly average of daily pain (WADP) score on the numerical rating scale (NRS) 11-point (0–10) scale.
To evaluate the treatment effect of PPS on knee pain and function in participants with knee OA pain.

• Change from baseline (CfB) at Day 112 in function as assessed by the average functional subscale score of the WOMAC NRS 3.1 Index (for EMA/MHRA, function will be included as a co- primaryprimary endpoint but still tested in hierarchical order).
• Change from baseline (CfB) at Day 112 in knee pain as assessed by the average pain subscale score of the WOMAC NRS 3.1 Index.
To evaluate the effect of PPS treatment on PGIC in participants with knee OA pain.
• Patient Global Impression of Change scores at Day 112.
To evaluate the efficacy of PPS treatment on knee pain, function, stiffness, and overall, in participants with knee OA pain.

Pain • Proportion of participants with ≥30% pain reduction as assessed by the average pain subscale score of the WOMAC NRS 3.1 Index at Day 112. Function • Proportion of participants with ≥30% improvement in function as assessed by the average functional subscale score of the WOMAC NRS 3.1 Index from baseline at Day 112.
To evaluate the efficacy of PPS treatment on knee pain, function, stiffness, and overall, in participants with knee OA pain.

Pain • Change from baseline of average weekly daily pain at Days 11, 25, 39, 56, 84, 140, 168, 224, 280, 336, and 404 in knee pain as assessed by weekly average of ADP on the NRS Index (11- point point scale). • Change from baseline at Days 11, 25, 39, 56, 84, 140, 168, 224, 280, 336, and 404 in knee pain as assessed by the average pain subscale score of the WOMAC NRS 3.1 Index. Function • Change from baseline at Days 11, 25, 39, 56, 84, 140, 168, 224, 280, 336, and 404 in function as assessed by the average functional subscale score of the WOMAC NRS 3.1 Index.
To evaluate the efficacy of PPS treatment on knee pain, function, stiffness, and overall, in participants with knee OA pain.

Pain • Proportion of participants with ≥50% pain reduction as assessed by the average pain subscale score of the WOMAC NRS 3.1 Index at Days 11, 25, 39, 56, 84, 112, 140, 168, 224, 280, 336, and 404. Function • Proportion of participants with ≥50% improvement in function as assessed by the average functional subscale score of the WOMAC NRS 3.1 Index from baseline at Days 11, 25, 39, 56, 84, 112, 140, 168, 224, 280, 336, and 404. Stiffness • Change from baseline at Days 11, 25, 39, 56, 84, 112, 140, 168, 224, 280, 336, and 404 in knee stiffness as assessed by the average stiffness subscale score of the WOMAC NRS 3.1 Index. Overall • Change from baseline at Days 11, 25, 39, 56, 84, 112, 140, 168, 224, 280, 336, and 404 overall as assessed by the overall score of WOMAC NRS 3.1 Index.
To evaluate the effect of PPS treatment on PGIC in participants with knee OA pain.
To evaluate the effect of PPS on rescue medication use in participants with knee OA pain.
• Number of days of rescue medication used from Day 1 to Days 39, 112 and 404.
To evaluate the effect of PPS on QoL in participants with knee OA pain.
• Change from baseline at Days 11, 25, 39, 56, 84, 112, 140, 168, 224, 280, 336, and 404 in QoL as assessed by EQ-5D-5L.
To evaluate the effect of PPS treatment on cartilage volume and thickness, joint synovitis/effusion, and BMLs.a

• Change in cartilage volume and thickness on MRI from baseline at Day 1 to Day 168 and 404. • Change in joint synovitis/effusion volume on MRI from baseline at Day 1 to Day 168 and 404. • Change in subchondral BML area and volume on MRI from baseline at Day 1 to Day 168 and 404. • Change in bone shape/osteophytes on MRI from baseline at Day 1 to Day 168 and 404.
To evaluate the effect of PPS treatment on joint space width.a
• Change in joint space width on X-ray from baseline at Day 1 to Day 404.
To evaluate the response rate to PPS treatment in participants with knee OA pain.
• OMERACT-OARSI Responder Index response rate at Days 39, 56, 84, 112, 140, 168, 224, 280, 336, and 404.
To evaluate the effect of PPS on work productivity and activity impairment (WPAI) in participants with knee OA pain.
• Change from baseline at Days 39, 56, 84, 112, 140, 168, 224, 280, 336, and 404 in WPAI questionnaire score.
To evaluate the safety and tolerability of PPS in participants with knee OA pain.
• Incidence of treatment-emergent AEs (TEAEs), including serious AEs (SAEs). • Treatment-emergent clinical laboratory abnormalities.
To evaluate the effect of anti-drug antibodies (ADAs) following PPS administration in participants with knee OA pain. (minimum 20 participants per group).
• Number of participants with an ADA response after treatment. • Correlation of ADA response with clinical events.
To characterise the PK profile of single and multiple doses in a subset of study participants (minimum 20 participants per group).
• PPS PK profile of single and multiple doses based on sparse blood sampling.
To evaluate the effect of PPS treatment on serum biomarkers.a
• CfB to Days 4, 8, 11, 15, 18, 25, 32, 39, 56, 84, and 168 in serum biomarkers includingwhich may include but not limited to: COMP, aggrecan ARGS, CRTAC-1, PRO-C2 PIIBNP, NTx-Ⅰ, and HA.
To explore the correlation of PPS treatment on subchondral BMLs, joint synovitis/effusion, cartilage volume, bone shape changes and joint space width, and clinical outcomes.a

• The effect of PPS on cartilage volume and thickness changes on MRI and whether these correlate with clinical outcomes. • The effect of PPS on joint synovitis/effusion volume on MRI and whether these correlate with clinical outcomes. • The effect of PPS on subchondral BML volume and area on MRI and whether these changes correlate with clinical outcomes. • The effect of PPS on bone shape changes and whether these correlate with clinical outcomes. • The effect of PPS on joint space width changes and whether these correlate with clinical outcomes.
2 mL vial, sterile solution for injection

Regular use of individualc vitamins and dietary supplements known to alter haemostasis including ajoene, birch bark, cayenne, Chinese black tree fungus, cumin, evening primrose oil, feverfew, garlic, ginger, ginkgo biloba, ginseng, grapeseed extract, milk thistle, omega 3 fatty acids, onion extract, St. John’s wort, turmeric, vitamins C and E, vitamin K

From 72 hours before Screening to Day 84
Three (3) consecutive days, no more than 4 days per week (7-day period).
Rescue medication taken: • Tues, Wed, Thurs, Sun is acceptable. • Tues, Wed, Thurs, Sun, Mon is not acceptable as it is more than 4 days within a 7-day period.

Oral Topical NSAIDs are allowed after Day 112 with a maximum 1-week course for non-index knee reasons but there is a requirement to stop a minimum of 1 week before the respective visit window to allow for each pain (ADP NRS) assessment.

Visits for Day 140, 168, 224, 280, 300 and Day 404 all have a visit window of +/-7 days. NSAIDs are not to be taken for 14 days prior to the visit given the weekly ADP NRS assessment is calculated from 7 days prior to visit.
Vitamin supplements (excluding vitamins C, E, K and biotin/B-7 taken as an individual supplement)
Topical analgesics: capsaicin ointment, lidocaine patch, and rubefacients (e.g.,. camphor, menthol, capsaicin, lidocaine) except for use on the index knee (Section 6.8.1)
Isolated small drusen are considered "Category 1" (No AMD) by AREDS and do not represent a pathological state.
Numerous small drusen or any medium 63–124
These alterations represent the "tipping point" toward pathology and may mimic the early hyper/hypo-autofluorescent patterns of PPS maculopathy.
High risk of progression and pathologic structural changes that likely would confound safety assessments.
End stage appearance with significant structural changes
Repeated use of non- narcotic pain reliever >24 hours or interferes with activity
Any use of narcotic pain reliever or prevents daily activity
2.5–5 cm and does not interfere with activity

All randomised participants who receive ≥1 dose(s) of IP. All safety analyses will use the Safety Analysis Set. Participants will be analysed according to the treatment actually received, regardless of the treatment assigned.

All randomizedrandomised participants who receive at least one dose of IP aligning with the (Intent to treat) ITT principle. Participants will be analyzedanalysed according to their planned randomizedrandomised treatment. This analysis set will be used for the analysis of efficacy and exploratory endpoints.

All participants in the FAS population who complete up to Day 112 without any major protocol deviation and received at least 9 doses of IP. This analysis set will be used to analyse selected efficacy endpoints. Participants will be analysed according to the randomised treatment. The PP participant determinations will be made prior to database lock.
All participants who have screening assessments taken (i.e. defined as those who have signed the informed consent formICF).
All participants who are randomizedrandomised into the study.

The PK analysis set consists of all participants who receive at least one dose of PPS and have at least one post-dose plasma concentration assessment. All PK analyses will be based on the PK population.
RBC indices: MCV, MCH, and MCHC
WBC count with differential: neutrophils, lymphocytes, monocytes, eosinophils, and basophils.
Plasma renin Concentration/plasma renin activity Cortisol-binding globulin

Synthetic steroid panel (serum)–betamethasone, budesonide, dexamethasone, fludrocortisone, megestrol acetate, methylprednisolone, prednisolone, prednisone, triamcinolone acetonide. Synthetic glucocorticoid steroid screen (urine)–betamethasone, budesonide, dexamethasone, fludrocortisone, megestrol acetate, methylprednisolone, prednisolone, prednisone, triamcinolone acetonide.

Specific gravity, pH, glucose, protein, blood, ketones, bilirubin, urobilinogen, nitrite, leukocytes by dipstick (local laboratory). Microscopic examination including erythrocytes, leukocytes, bacteria, casts, epithelial cells, and crystals if dipstick is positive for leukocytes and/or blood/haemoglobin.
hCGHCG pregnancy test (as needed for WOCBP) in serum (central laboratory) and urine (local laboratory).
Anti-PF4/SRA D-dimer Fibrinogen FSH and oestradiol (as needed in WONCBP only)). Serology (HIV antibody, HBsAg, and HCV antibody)).
